---
title: "A2 - Version Control with Git & GitHub"
output: html_document
---

# A2 - Version Control with Git & GitHub

## Useful References
- [Pro Git Book](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2)
- [GitHub Docs for Beginners](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started)

## Learning Objectives
In this session we will cover:
- Tracking changes to scripts with Git.
- Creating commits that describe your scientific decisions.
- Pushing to GitHub for collaboration and backup.

## Why Git for Biology?
Instead of naming files `script_final.R`, `script_final_v2_USE_THIS_ONE.R`, Git allows you to maintain one file (`script.R`) and tracks its entire history through safe "save points" (commits).

## Basic Commands
We can run git commands from the RStudio terminal (`Terminal` tab next to `Console`), or regular Terminal. 

```{bash git-init, eval=FALSE}
# 1. Ask git to track the current project directory
git init

# 2. Check the current status (what changed?)
git status

# 3. Stage the files you want to save
git add analysis_script.R

# 4. Create a "save point" with a descriptive message
git commit -m "Added DESeq2 normalization logic"
```

## Remote Repositories (GitHub)
Once tracked locally, we back up the project to GitHub. This ensures the data science code lives safely in the cloud.

```{bash git-push, eval=FALSE}
# Link the local project to an empty GitHub repository
git remote add origin https://github.com/Username/My-Bio-Project.git

# Push your history and files to the cloud
git push -u origin main
```

**Pro Tip:** Never commit large `.csv` files or raw FASTQ data to git. Always create a `.gitignore` file and type `data/` inside it to keep your repository light!
